scenario_title: Add RR beneath an existing CNAME owner name
test_rationale: This test creates a minimal zone where alias.campus.edu. is already a pure CNAME owner. The single UPDATE tries to add an A RR at that same owner name, which is exactly the underspecified case: the protocol forbids CNAME coexistence but does not define how dynamic update must enforce it. The follow-up queries to alias.campus.edu. for both CNAME and A make it possible to distinguish strict rejection from permissive or repair-style behavior by comparing the resulting rrsets and reply code.

group_1.behavior: Reject the UPDATE because adding a non-CNAME RR at a CNAME owner violates exclusivity; the existing CNAME remains intact.
group_1.post_state: alias.campus.edu. continues to have only the CNAME rrset; no A rrset is created.

group_2.behavior: Accept the UPDATE or otherwise handle the conflict by altering the name contents, resulting in a different on-wire reply and/or name state.
group_2.post_state: alias.campus.edu. may end up with an A rrset, or with the CNAME removed/replaced, rather than the original pure CNAME state.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 4035 §2.5 and RFC 2181 §10.1 forbid coexistence of a CNAME with other data, but RFC 2136 §3.4.2 does not specify the required UPDATE error handling when add_rr targets an owner name that already has a CNAME. Implementations therefore may reject the UPDATE with YXRRSET/SERVFAIL or may accept and mutate the name contents, producing different replies and/or post-update rrsets.
